response:
The quadratic equation for the batting score is \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). To find the maximum score we locate the vertex of this parabola. The vertex occurs at \( n = -b / (2a) \) where \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \). Calculating this value gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) \). This simplifies to \( n = -5 / -0.4 \). The result is exactly twelve point five practice sessions per month. Since the number of sessions must be a whole number we check integers near this value. Evaluating the function at \( n = 12 \) yields \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 \). This equals eighty one point two runs. Evaluating the function at \( n = 13 \) yields \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 \). This equals eighty one point two runs as well. Both values produce the same maximum average batting score of eighty one point two.

Next we calculate the win probability for five practice sessions. First we find the score at \( n = 5 \). Substituting into the equation gives \( S = 50 + 25 - 5 \). The score is seventy runs. Plugging this into the logistic formula gives \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1 \times 70 - 3)}) \). The exponent becomes \( -(7 - 3) \) which is negative four. So \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \). This value is approximately zero point nine eight two zero. Now we calculate the score for ten practice sessions. Substituting \( n = 10 \) gives \( S = 50 + 50 - 20 \). The score is eighty runs. Plugging this into the logistic formula gives \( P_{10} = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1 \times 80 - 3)}) \). The exponent becomes \( -(8 - 3) \) which is negative five. So \( P_{10} = 1 / (1 + e^{-5}) \). This value is approximately zero point nine nine three three. The change in probability is the difference
